long_reaction_chains.name: Long Reaction Chains
long_reaction_chains.description: Questions involving multiple sequential steps, reagents, or transformations.

structural_patterns: Product of one step becomes reactant of next

counting_enumeration.name: Counting/Enumeration
counting_enumeration.description: Questions requiring counting of molecules, ions, bonds, or specific numerical calculations.

deep_mechanistic_reasoning.name: Deep Mechanistic Reasoning
deep_mechanistic_reasoning.description: Questions requiring understanding of reaction mechanisms, intermediates, or transition states.

comparative_ranking.name: Comparative Ranking
comparative_ranking.description: Questions requiring comparison of properties (acidity, basicity, reactivity, etc.) and arranging in orders.

questions.question_text: An organic compound contains C, H and N atoms in ratio 9 : 1 : 3.5 by weight. If its molar mass is 108 g mol⁻¹, molecular formula will be: a) C₆H₈N₂ b) C₇H₁₀N c) C₅H₆N₃ d) C₄H₁₈N₃
questions.solution_summary: Weight ratio C:H:N = 9:1:3.5. Mole ratio = 9/12 : 1/1 : 3.5/14 = 0.75 : 1 : 0.25 = 3:4:1. Empirical formula = C₃H₄N. Empirical mass = 36+4+14 = 54. n = 108/54 = 2. Molecular formula = C₆H₈N₂.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Conversion of weight ratio to mole ratio, determination of empirical formula, then molecular formula.

questions.question_text: 5 moles of AB₂ weighs 125 g and 10 moles of A₂B₂ weighs 300 g. The molar mass of A and B (in g mol⁻¹) respectively are: a) 10, 5 b) 50, 25 c) 25, 50 d) 5, 10
questions.solution_summary: Molar mass of AB₂ = 125/5 = 25 g mol⁻¹. Molar mass of A₂B₂ = 300/10 = 30 g mol⁻¹. Let molar mass of A = a, B = b. a + 2b = 25, 2a + 2b = 30. Solving: a = 5, b = 10.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Solving simultaneous equations for molar masses.

questions.question_text: The number of angular nodes and radial nodes in 3d orbital respectively are: a) 0, 3 b) 2, 0 c) 3, 2 d) 2, 1
questions.solution_summary: For d orbital (l=2): angular nodes = l = 2. Radial nodes = n - l - 1 = 3 - 2 - 1 = 0.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Application of formulas for angular and radial nodes.

questions.question_text: Consider the following molecules: (I) CO₂ (II) H₂O (III) BF₃ (IV) NH₃. Out of these, molecule(s) having sp³ hybridization of central atom is/are: a) I, II, III, IV b) I, II c) II, IV d) IV only
questions.solution_summary: CO₂: sp (linear). H₂O: sp³ (bent, 4 electron pairs). BF₃: sp² (trigonal planar). NH₃: sp³ (pyramidal, 4 electron pairs). So H₂O and NH₃ have sp³.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Identifying hybridisation based on electron pair geometry.

questions.question_text: Which of the following molecule is paramagnetic having bond order two? a) C₂ b) N₂ c) O₂ d) F₂
questions.solution_summary: O₂: bond order = 2, has 2 unpaired electrons (paramagnetic). C₂: bond order = 2, diamagnetic. N₂: bond order = 3, diamagnetic. F₂: bond order = 1, diamagnetic.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Application of MO theory to determine bond order and magnetic property.

questions.question_text: What will be enthalpy of following reaction: C(graphite,s) + ½O₂(g) → CO(g). Given: C(graphite,s) + O₂(g) → CO₂(g) ; ΔrH° = -393.5 kJ mol⁻¹. CO(g) + ½O₂(g) → CO₂(g) ; ΔrH° = -283.0 kJ mol⁻¹. a) -676.5 kJ mol⁻¹ b) -110.5 kJ mol⁻¹ c) -196.75 kJ mol⁻¹ d) 110.5 kJ mol⁻¹
questions.solution_summary: Reverse second equation: CO₂(g) → CO(g) + ½O₂(g); ΔH = +283.0 kJ. Add first equation: C + O₂ → CO₂; ΔH = -393.5 kJ. Net: C + ½O₂ → CO; ΔH = -393.5 + 283.0 = -110.5 kJ mol⁻¹.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Application of Hess's law.

questions.question_text: For an isolated system: a) ΔU = 0, ΔS = 0 b) ΔU = 0, ΔS > 0 c) ΔU > 0, ΔS < 0 d) ΔU < 0, ΔS = 0
questions.solution_summary: For isolated system, no energy or matter exchange: ΔU = 0. For spontaneous process in isolated system, entropy increases: ΔS > 0.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Understanding of isolated systems and entropy change.

questions.question_text: For the equilibrium, 2X(g) ⇌ 2Y(s) + Z(g); Kc = 300 and ΔrH = -100 kJ/mol. Kp at 300 K will be: a) 9×10⁴R b) 300R c) R d) 1/R (R is universal gas constant)
questions.solution_summary: Kp = Kc(RT)^Δn. Δn = (moles gaseous products) - (moles gaseous reactants) = 1 - 2 = -1. Kp = Kc(RT)^-1 = 300/(RT) = 300/R × 1/T. At 300K, Kp = 300/(R×300) = 1/R. Wait, let's recalculate: Δn = 1 - 2 = -1. Kp = Kc(RT)^-1 = Kc/(RT) = 300/(R×300) = 1/R. So Kp = 1/R. Option (d). But option (a) says 9×10⁴R. Let me check: Maybe Δn = 1 - 2 = -1. Kp = Kc(RT)^-1 = 300/(R×300) = 1/R. So answer is (d).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Application of Kp = Kc(RT)^Δn.

questions.question_text: The best suitable method (sequence of reactions) to convert benzene into p-Chlorotoluene is: a) 1. CH₃Cl / anhydrous AlCl₃ 2. CH₄ / H⁺ b) 1. Cl₂ / anhydrous AlCl₃ 2. CH₃Cl / anhydrous AlCl₃ c) 1. CH₃Cl / anhydrous AlCl₃ 2. Cl₂ / anhydrous AlCl₃ d) 1. CH₃Cl / H⁺ 2. conc. HCl
questions.solution_summary: First Friedel-Crafts alkylation with CH₃Cl/AlCl₃ gives toluene (CH₃ is o,p-directing). Then chlorination with Cl₂/AlCl₃ gives p-chlorotoluene (para product favoured).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Understanding of directing effects in electrophilic substitution.

questions.question_text: The major product formed on reaction of 1-Methylcyclohex-1-ene with water in presence of dilute H₂SO₄ will be: a) 1-Methylcyclohexan-1-ol b) 2-Methylcyclohexan-1-ol c) 1-Methylcyclohex-2-ene d) 1-Methylcyclohexane
questions.solution_summary: Hydration of alkene follows Markovnikov rule. H adds to less substituted carbon, OH to more substituted carbon (C1, which already has CH3). Product: 1-methylcyclohexan-1-ol.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Application of Markovnikov rule for acid-catalysed hydration.

questions.question_text: Oxidation state of Mn in MnO₄⁻ is: a) 2 b) 5 c) 6 d) 7

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Calculation of oxidation state using standard rules.

questions.question_text: The IUPAC name of following compound is: (image: HO-CH₂-CH(CH₃)-CH₂-COOH) a) 4,4-Dimethyl-3-hydroxybutanoic acid b) 2-Methyl-3-hydroxypentan-5-oic acid c) 3-Hydroxy-4-methylpentanoic acid d) 4-Methyl-3-hydroxypentanoic acid
questions.solution_summary: Longest chain containing COOH has 5 carbons: pentanoic acid. OH at C3, methyl at C4. So 3-hydroxy-4-methylpentanoic acid.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Application of IUPAC nomenclature rules.

questions.question_text: Assertion: Cyclopentadienyl anion is aromatic. Reason: Cyclopentadienyl anion contains 6 π electrons. a) Both assertion and reason are correct and reason is correct explanation of assertion b) Both assertion and reason are correct but reason is not correct explanation of assertion c) Assertion is correct but reason is incorrect d) Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct
questions.solution_summary: Cyclopentadienyl anion (C₅H₅⁻) has 6 π electrons (4n+2, n=1), is planar and conjugated, so aromatic. Reason correctly explains assertion.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Understanding of Huckel's rule for aromaticity.

questions.question_text: Assertion: Heat(q) and work(w) are not state function but q+w is state function. Reason: Sum of heat and work gives ΔU. a) Both assertion and reason are correct and reason is correct explanation of assertion b) Both assertion and reason are correct but reason is not correct explanation of assertion c) Assertion is correct but reason is incorrect d) Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct
questions.solution_summary: q and w are path functions, but q+w = ΔU which is a state function. Reason correctly explains the assertion.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Understanding of state functions vs path functions.

questions.question_text: Assertion: Halogens exhibits negative oxidation state. Reason: Halogens have high electronegativity. a) Both assertion and reason are correct and reason is correct explanation of assertion b) Both assertion and reason are correct but reason is not correct explanation of assertion c) Assertion is correct but reason is incorrect d) Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct
questions.solution_summary: Halogens show -1 oxidation state due to high electronegativity (tendency to gain electron). Reason correctly explains assertion.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Understanding of electronegativity and oxidation states.

questions.question_text: Assertion: Chlorine has most negative electron gain enthalpy among halogens. Reason: Chlorine has smallest size and highest electronegativity. a) Both assertion and reason are correct and reason is correct explanation of assertion b) Both assertion and reason are correct but reason is not correct explanation of assertion c) Assertion is correct but reason is incorrect d) Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct
questions.solution_summary: Assertion is true: Cl has most negative electron gain enthalpy. Reason is false: F has smallest size and highest electronegativity, but Cl has more negative electron gain enthalpy due to less electron-electron repulsion.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Understanding of electron gain enthalpy trend in halogens.

questions.question_text: Balance the following redox reaction in acidic medium: MnO₄⁻(aq) + C₂O₄²⁻(aq) → Mn²⁺(aq) + CO₂(g)
questions.solution_summary: Balanced equation using half-reaction method.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Balancing redox reactions in acidic medium.

questions.question_text: a) State law of multiple proportions. b) How 0.5 mol Na₂CO₃ and 0.5 M Na₂CO₃ are different?
questions.sub_topic: Law of Multiple Proportions, Mole vs Molarity
questions.correct_answer: a) Law of multiple proportions: When two elements combine to form more than one compound, the masses of one element that combine with a fixed mass of the other are in simple whole number ratio. b) 0.5 mol Na₂CO₃ is amount of substance (moles). 0.5 M Na₂CO₃ is concentration (molarity), i.e., 0.5 mol per litre of solution.
questions.solution_summary: Definitions and distinction.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of law and distinction between mole and molarity.

questions.question_text: a) What do you understand by isoelectronic species? b) Write a cation and anion which is isoelectronic with Ar?
questions.correct_answer: a) Isoelectronic species are atoms or ions that have the same number of electrons. b) Ar has 18 electrons. Cation: K⁺ (18 electrons). Anion: Cl⁻ (18 electrons).
questions.solution_summary: Definition and examples.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Definition and examples of isoelectronic species.

questions.question_text: a) State Le Chatelier principle. b) Write conjugate acid and base of HCO₃⁻ ion.
questions.sub_topic: Le Chatelier's Principle, Conjugate Acid-Base Pairs
questions.correct_answer: a) Le Chatelier's principle: If a system at equilibrium is subjected to a change in concentration, temperature, or pressure, the equilibrium shifts in a direction that counteracts the change. b) Conjugate acid: H₂CO₃. Conjugate base: CO₃²⁻.
questions.solution_summary: Statement of principle and conjugate acid-base pairs.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of Le Chatelier's principle and conjugate acid-base concept.

questions.question_text: Explain positive resonance effect using resonance structures of appropriate compound. OR Explain the principle involved in: a) Distillation b) Chromatography
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Basic Concepts / Purification Methods
questions.correct_answer: Positive resonance effect: Delocalisation of electrons towards the attacking reagent. Example: Aniline (NH₂ group donates electrons to ring). OR: a) Distillation: Separation based on difference in boiling points. b) Chromatography: Separation based on differential adsorption on stationary phase.
questions.solution_summary: Explanation of +R effect or principles of purification methods.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Understanding of resonance effect or purification techniques.

questions.question_text: A compound contains 4.07% hydrogen, 24.27% carbon and 71.65% chlorine. Its molar mass is 98.96 g. What are its empirical and molecular formulas?
questions.correct_answer: Empirical formula: CHCl. Molecular formula: C₂H₂Cl₂.
questions.solution_summary: Mole ratio: H=4.07/1=4.07, C=24.27/12=2.0225, Cl=71.65/35.5=2.018. Ratio = 2.0225:4.07:2.018 ≈ 1:2:1. Empirical formula = CH₂Cl. Empirical mass = 12+2+35.5=49.5. n = 98.96/49.5 = 2. Molecular formula = C₂H₄Cl₂.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Calculation of empirical and molecular formula from percentage composition.

questions.question_text: a) Write the IUPAC names of following (Any 2): i) CH₃-CH=CH-CH₂-OH ii) (image: structure) b) Write chain isomers of pentane.
questions.correct_answer: a) i) But-2-en-1-ol. ii) (based on image) likely 2-methylpropanoic acid or similar. b) Pentane isomers: n-pentane, isopentane (2-methylbutane), neopentane (2,2-dimethylpropane).
questions.solution_summary: IUPAC nomenclature and chain isomers.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: IUPAC nomenclature and isomerism.

questions.question_text: Calculate the energy associated with the first orbit of He⁺. What is the radius of this orbit?
questions.solution_summary: Application of Bohr's equations for hydrogen-like species.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Application of Bohr's formula for hydrogen-like atoms.

questions.question_text: Name the period-2 element which has: a) lowest ionization enthalpy b) positive electron gain enthalpy c) highest electronegativity
questions.correct_answer: a) Lithium (Li) - lowest ionisation enthalpy in period 2. b) Nitrogen (N) - positive electron gain enthalpy due to half-filled p³ configuration. c) Fluorine (F) - highest electronegativity.
questions.solution_summary: Elements based on periodic trends.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of periodic trends in period 2.

questions.question_text: Define following terms: a) Closed system b) Intensive property c) Entropy
questions.correct_answer: a) Closed system: exchanges energy but not matter with surroundings. b) Intensive property: independent of amount of substance (e.g., temperature, pressure, density). c) Entropy: measure of randomness or disorder of a system.
questions.solution_summary: Definitions of thermodynamic terms.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of thermodynamic definitions.

questions.question_text: For the reaction, 2A(g) + B(g) → D(g); ΔU° = -10.5 kJ, ΔS° = -44 J K⁻¹. Calculate ΔG° for the reaction, and predict whether the reaction may occur spontaneously.
questions.solution_summary: Calculation of ΔG from ΔU and ΔS, and predicting spontaneity.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Application of ΔH = ΔU + Δn_g RT and Gibbs free energy equation.

questions.question_text: But-2-yne on hydrogenation in presence of Pd-C forms X, while on reaction with Na in liquid NH₃ forms Y. a) Write structures of X and Y. b) Out of X and Y which have higher boiling point and why?
questions.sub_topic: Hydrogenation of Alkynes - Syn vs Anti Addition
questions.correct_answer: a) X = But-2-ene (cis) [Pd-C gives cis hydrogenation]. Y = But-2-ene (trans) [Na/liq NH₃ gives trans hydrogenation]. b) cis-but-2-ene has higher boiling point due to polar nature (net dipole moment), while trans is non-polar.
questions.solution_summary: Products of hydrogenation and comparison of boiling points.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Understanding of syn vs anti addition and effect on boiling points.

questions.question_text: Case: Organic Reactions. a) Write the intermediate formed in chlorination of ethane. b) Select the electrophile out of: H₂O, NH₃, NF₃, BF₃. c) Arrange following carbocations in increasing order of stability: CH₃CH₂⁺, (CH₃)₂CH⁺, O₂N-CH₂CH₂⁺. OR Explain two types of bond fission with examples.
questions.correct_answer: a) Ethyl free radical (CH₃CH₂•). b) BF₃ (electron deficient, Lewis acid). c) O₂N-CH₂CH₂⁺ < CH₃CH₂⁺ < (CH₃)₂CH⁺ (nitro group destabilises). OR: Homolytic fission (free radicals, e.g., Cl₂ → 2Cl•) and Heterolytic fission (carbocation/carbanion, e.g., R-Cl → R⁺ + Cl⁻).
questions.solution_summary: Intermediates, electrophile identification, and carbocation stability order.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Understanding of reaction intermediates and factors affecting stability.

questions.question_text: Case: pH and Buffer Solutions. a) Give an example of salt formed by reaction of a strong acid and weak base. b) Give example of basic buffer solution. c) Calculate the pH of a 0.001 M NaOH solution. OR Explain common ion effect with appropriate example.
questions.sub_topic: pH, Buffer Solutions, Common Ion Effect
questions.correct_answer: a) NH₄Cl (strong acid HCl + weak base NH₃). b) NH₄OH + NH₄Cl (weak base + its salt). c) [OH⁻] = 1×10⁻³ M, pOH = 3, pH = 11. OR: Common ion effect: suppression of ionisation of weak electrolyte by adding a strong electrolyte containing common ion. Example: CH₃COOH + CH₃COONa (common CH₃COO⁻).
questions.solution_summary: Examples and pH calculation.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Understanding of salts, buffers, pH calculation, and common ion effect.

questions.question_text: a) Complete the reactions by writing structures of A to F. (Image: reaction sequence) b) Explain Wurtz reaction with appropriate example. Why alkanes containing odd number of carbon atoms cannot be synthesised by this reaction? OR a) Write Friedel-Crafts alkylation reaction and explain its mechanism. b) Write reactions for: i) Benzene to m-nitroacetophenone ii) Ethyne to toluene
questions.correct_answer: b) Wurtz reaction: 2R-X + 2Na → R-R + 2NaX. Example: 2CH₃Br + 2Na → CH₃CH₃. Odd number of carbon alkanes cannot be prepared because coupling of two identical alkyl groups gives even number of carbons. OR: a) Friedel-Crafts alkylation: Benzene + R-Cl → Alkylbenzene (AlCl₃). Mechanism: formation of carbocation, electrophilic attack on benzene, deprotonation. b) i) Benzene → acetophenone (Friedel-Crafts acylation) → nitration → m-nitroacetophenone. ii) Ethyne → benzene (cyclic polymerisation) → Friedel-Crafts alkylation with CH₃Cl → toluene.
questions.solution_summary: Reaction sequences, Wurtz reaction, and conversions.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Application of multiple organic reactions and conversions.

questions.question_text: a) Write molecular orbital electronic configurations of N₂ molecule. Calculate its bond order and comment on its magnetic behaviour. b) Explain shapes of following molecules on the basis of VSEPR theory: XeF₄, ClF₃, NH₃. OR a) Explain: i) NH₃ has higher boiling point than PH₃. ii) He₂ molecule does not exist. iii) BF₃ molecule is non-polar. b) Explain hybridisation of phosphorus in PCl₅ molecule.
questions.correct_answer: a) N₂: KK(σ2s)²(σ*2s)²(π2px²=π2py²)(σ2pz²). Bond order = 3 (triple bond). Diamagnetic. b) XeF₄: square planar (sp³d²). ClF₃: T-shaped (sp³d). NH₃: pyramidal (sp³). OR: a) i) NH₃ has H-bonding, PH₃ doesn't. ii) He₂: bond order = 0 (σ1s² σ*1s²), so doesn't exist. iii) BF₃: trigonal planar, dipole moments cancel. b) PCl₅: sp³d hybridisation, trigonal bipyramidal.
questions.solution_summary: MO configuration, VSEPR shapes, and explanations.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Application of MO theory, VSEPR theory, and explanation of properties.

questions.question_text: a) Draw the shape of dxy orbital. b) How many subshells are associated with n=3? c) Write the electronic configuration of Cr (Z=24). Why is it different from the expected configuration? d) What will be the wavelength of a ball of mass 100 g moving with velocity 10 m s⁻¹? OR a) State Pauli's exclusion principle. b) Write one difference between principal quantum number and azimuthal quantum number. c) A golf ball has mass 40g and speed 45 m/s. If speed can be measured within accuracy of 2%, calculate uncertainty in position.
questions.sub_topic: Orbital Shapes, Electronic Configuration, de Broglie Wavelength, Uncertainty Principle
questions.correct_answer: a) dxy orbital has four lobes between axes. b) 3 subshells (s, p, d). c) Cr: [Ar]3d⁵4s¹ (expected [Ar]3d⁴4s²). Difference due to extra stability of half-filled d⁵. d) λ = h/(mv) = 6.626×10⁻³⁴/(0.1×10) = 6.626×10⁻³⁴ m. OR: a) Pauli exclusion: no two electrons can have same set of four quantum numbers. b) n: shell/energy, l: subshell/shape. c) Δx = h/(4πmΔv) = 6.626×10⁻³⁴/(4π×0.04×0.02×45) ≈ 1.46×10⁻³² m.
questions.solution_summary: Multiple atomic structure concepts.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Multiple concepts from atomic structure.
